plainMeaning: The Jina is honoured on every side — by demons and divine birds, by celestial musicians and serpent-beings, by countless gods, and by the whole community of monks.
recitation: asura garula parivaṁdiaṁ, kinnaroraga ṇamaṁsiaṁ; devakoḍi saya saṁthuaṁ, samaṇa saṁgha parivaṁdiaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: asura (a class of titan/demigod, the bhavanavāsī demon-lords)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. asura; Prakrit asura, unchanged. Here in compound with the following word.
wordByWord.notes: First of the paired venerators; nominally the lower, fierce divinities.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. garuḍa → Prakrit garula, by the regular shift of intervocalic ḍ → l.
wordByWord.notes: asura-garula forms a dvandva (asuras-and-garuḍas) governing parivaṁdiaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: venerated all around, saluted on every side
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. parivandita, past participle of pari (around, fully) + √vand (to salute/extol); Prakrit -ita → -ia, accusative sg. -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative participle qualifying the (yet-unnamed) Jina; the prefix pari- adds the sense of circumambulating, total homage.

wordByWord.gloss: kinnaras and uragas (celestial musicians and serpent-beings/nāgas)
wordByWord.etymology: kinnara + uraga (Skt. uraga, 'breast-goer,' a serpent); sandhi kinnara+uraga → kinnaroraga; both are vyantara semi-divine classes.
wordByWord.notes: A second dvandva pair, governing ṇamaṁsiaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. namasita / namasyamāna, from √nam (to bow) via the denominative namasya-; Prakrit initial n- → ṇ-, accusative sg. -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Second of the four veneration-participles; specifically the act of bowing/prostration.

wordByWord.etymology: deva (god) + koḍi (Skt. koṭi, 'ten million, crore'); Prakrit koṭi → koḍi by ṭ → ḍ softening.
wordByWord.notes: Refers chiefly to the vaimānika heaven-dwelling gods; with saya it yields 'hundreds of crores.'

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śata → Prakrit saya, with ś → s and intervocalic t → y.
wordByWord.notes: Multiplier with devakoḍi — a hyperbolic 'hundreds of crores,' i.e. innumerable.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saṁstuta, past participle of sam (fully) + √stu (to praise); Prakrit -st- → -tth-/-th-, accusative sg. -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Third veneration-participle; from the same √stu that gives 'stava,' the genre of this hymn.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śramaṇa ('one who exerts/strives') → Prakrit samaṇa, ś → s.
Skt. saṁgha (sam + √han/√gam, 'that which is gathered together'); Prakrit saṁgha, unchanged.
wordByWord.notes: samaṇa-saṁgha = the assembly of mendicants; representing the saṅgha that honours the Jina.

wordByWord.gloss: venerated all around, saluted on every side
wordByWord.etymology: As above: pari + √vand, Skt. parivandita → Prakrit parivaṁdia, accusative sg. -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberate repetition of the opening word, bracketing the verse: it begins and ends with 'venerated all around.'

literalTranslation: Venerated all around by asuras and garuḍas, bowed down to by kinnaras and uragas, praised by hundreds of crores of gods, venerated all around by the community of śramaṇas (—him I revere).
idiomaticTranslation: Hailed on every side by demon-lords and the divine eagles, bowed to by the heavenly minstrels and the serpent-folk, hymned by gods in their hundreds of crores, and adored by the whole assembly of monks — such is the Jina, worshipped through every realm of being.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza is one unbroken garland of four accusative participles — parivaṁdiaṁ, ṇamaṁsiaṁ, saṁthuaṁ, parivaṁdiaṁ — each ending in -aṁ and all left grammatically 'hanging' until the very next verse seizes them with 'ajiaṁ ajiaṁ payao paṇame' ('him, the Unconquered, I reverently bow to'). The reciter should feel the suspension: the epithets pile up here and are resolved only in the following line. The poet arranges the worshippers as an ascending census of the universe. First the fierce and animal-totem divinities (asuras, garuḍas); then the gentler semi-divine vyantaras (kinnara musicians, uraga serpent-folk); then the heaven-gods, numbered with deliberate hyperbole as 'hundreds of crores'; and — climactically, in the final quarter — not gods at all, but the samaṇa-saṁgha, the human community of renunciants. That the verse opens and closes on the same word, parivaṁdiaṁ, frames the whole cosmos within a single act of homage. Note too the variation of verbs: circumambulating salute, prostration, sung praise, and salute again — devotion expressed bodily, vocally, and communally. The placement of the monastic order at the summit, above the massed gods, is itself the message: in the Jain order of worth, the striving ascetic outranks the celestial.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse paints in miniature the samavasaraṇa, the divine preaching-assembly where, by Jain cosmology, all four classes of gods — bhavanavāsī (here the asuras), vyantara (kinnaras, uragas), jyotiṣka, and vaimānika (the 'crores of gods') — together with humans and animals gather to honour a Tīrthaṅkara. The Jina neither asks for nor needs this homage; their vītarāga (passion-free) perfection simply draws all sentient orders to reverence. By ending on the śramaṇa-saṅgha, the stanza affirms a cardinal Jain valuation: worship offered by one treading the path of self-conquest is weightier than that of any heaven-dweller, for the gods enjoy merit but cannot, while gods, attain liberation.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited aloud, this verse invites the practitioner to place their own small bow within an immense, universe-wide chorus of veneration — dissolving ego by joining demons, serpents, gods, and monks in one shared act. The lesson of its climax is personal: aspire not to the gods' splendour but to the monk's striving, since only that road leads to the unconquered peace the hymn seeks.

crossReferences.text: Following verse: 'abhayaṁ aṇahaṁ arayaṁ aruaṁ, ajiaṁ ajiaṁ payao paṇame' — which grammatically completes this verse's dangling epithets.
The twelve-fold parṣadā of the samavasaraṇa (four god-classes, ascetics, and laity assembled before the Jina).
crossReferences.source: Jain cosmological tradition; cf. Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacarita I

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (entries: vand, namasya, stu/saṁstuta, garuḍa, koṭi, śramaṇa, saṅgha)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots and chāyā underlying parivandita, namasita, saṁstuta, garuḍa, koṭi, śramaṇa, saṅgha.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit-Hindi dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms garula (<garuḍa), koḍi (<koṭi), saya (<śata), saṁthua (<saṁstuta), ṇamaṁsia, parivaṁdia.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary — Ajitaśānti Stava texts and stotra anthologies
sources.consultedFor: Verse text, attribution to Nandiṣeṇasūri, and the cosmic-assembly veneration motif of the hymn.

notes: The four -aṁ words (parivaṁdiaṁ, ṇamaṁsiaṁ, saṁthuaṁ, parivaṁdiaṁ) are accusative singular past participles that syntactically depend on the following verse's 'ajiaṁ ... paṇame'; this verse is therefore not a standalone sentence but the first half of a homage completed in v.21. 'kinnaroraga' is read as the sandhi-compound kinnara+uraga (uraga = serpent-being / nāga); some renderings gloss uraga loosely as 'nāga,' which is equivalent here. 'devakoḍi saya' is hyperbolic ('hundreds of crores'), not a precise count. Orthography (anusvāra, single/double conjuncts) preserved per the source book; no variant readings were flagged for this verse in the provided context.
